aliases: Indian Potato and Green Pea Curry

english: Indian Potato and Green Pea Curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for aloo matar exists; mapped to 'potato dish' (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific curry-based vegetable dish term is absent in current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers potato-based dishes broadly. No specific aloo matar or Indian potato-pea curry code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue; closest parent code used as the most defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for aloo matar or equivalent Indian vegetable curry identified in current release. Null retained to avoid code fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of aloo matar is approximately 250 g, presented as a bowl of potato and green pea curry accompanied by rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Aloo Matar (Potato and Peas Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for potato curry and green peas curry as separate entries. Values for aloo matar have been derived by combining the ICMR-NIN potato curry (boiled potato in spiced gravy) and green peas curry entries in approximate proportions consistent with the standard recipe (roughly 60% potato, 25% green peas, 15% gravy base), then cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for boiled potato (FDC ID 170026) and cooked green peas (FDC ID 170420) to validate macro plausibility. The composite estimate is classified as 'adapted'.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for aloo matar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the GI of boiled potato (GI ~65, University of Sydney GI Database, reference food: boiled white potato) as the dominant ingredient (~60% by weight). Green peas have a low GI (~48); the blended dish GI is estimated conservatively at 65 reflecting the potato-dominant carbohydrate profile. Glycemic load calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 30.0) / 100 = 19.5, using 250 g serving with 12.0 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 30.0 g available carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Aloo matar contains approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (700 mg per standard 250 g serving), which represents about 30% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by potato content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 65 and a glycemic load of approximately 19.5 per standard serving, aloo matar poses a moderate postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; pairing with a high-fibre bread such as whole-wheat roti or reducing portion size to 150 g can lower the effective glycemic load to approximately 12.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo matar is cholesterol-free and contains only 0.3 g saturated fat per 100 g, making it a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal oil; the green peas contribute dietary fibre (2.4 g per 100 g) and potassium (310 mg per 100 g), both of which support cardiovascular health.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Matar (also spelled Aloo Mutter, Alu Matar, or Aloo Mattar) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry made from diced or cubed potatoes (aloo) and green peas (matar) cooked together in a spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first tempering whole spices such as cumin seeds in oil, then sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic until golden, adding tomatoes and ground spices including turmeric, coriander, cumin, and garam masala, and finally simmering the potatoes and peas until tender. The result is a mildly spiced, slightly tangy curry with a soft, starchy texture from the potatoes and a sweet, earthy note from the peas. Aloo matar is a staple of everyday North Indian home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, the United Kingdom, and the broader South Asian diaspora. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Nutritionally, it provides moderate carbohydrate energy (82 kcal per 100 g), useful dietary fibre (2.4 g per 100 g), and meaningful potassium (310 mg per 100 g). The medium glycemic index (~65) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate portion size or pair the dish with lower-GI accompaniments. Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension. The dish is low in saturated fat and free of cholesterol, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular health when prepared with modest oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in aloo matar?
Is aloo matar good for diabetics?
What is the GI of aloo matar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using a composite of potato curry and green peas curry entries, cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data. No single exact entry for aloo matar exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled potato as the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a home-style or restaurant-style aloo matar with moderate oil use (~1–2 tsp per serving). Restaurant versions in Singapore (field survey listings) may use more oil or cream, which would increase energy, fat, and saturated fat values. 2. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products — confirmed zero by ingredient composition. Vitamin D is 0.0 as no fortified ingredients are used and the dish contains no animal-derived vitamin D sources — confirmed zero by ingredient composition. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in standard preparation — confirmed zero. Cholesterol is 0.0 as the dish is entirely plant-based — naturally zero. 3. GI estimate of 65 is based on boiled potato as the dominant carbohydrate source; actual GI may vary depending on potato variety, cooking time, and cooling (retrograded starch lowers GI). 4. Sodium value of 280 mg per 100 g reflects moderate restaurant-style salting; home preparations may be lower (~150–200 mg per 100 g). 5. The food_name canonical form used is 'Aloo Matar' (two words, standard Hindi romanization); 'Aloo Muttar' from the input is retained as an alias.
